articles.title: BBC Admits Editorial Breach Over Racial Slur in Baftas Broadcast
articles.summary: The BBC's executive complaints unit (ECU) has upheld complaints that the broadcast of a racial slur during the Bafta Film Awards breached editorial standards. In February, a Tourette's campaigner involuntarily shouted the n-word while actors Michael B Jordan and Delroy Lindo presented an award. The slur was not edited out of the delayed TV broadcast on BBC One and remained available on iPlayer until the next morning, leading to numerous complaints. The ECU found the inclusion highly offensive with no editorial justification, constituting a breach, but determined it was unintentional as the production team did not hear the word at the time. However, they noted that leaving the unedited version on iPlayer overnight aggravated the offence. BBC chief content officer Kate Phillips acknowledged the serious mistake and outlined measures to improve pre-event planning, live production, and iPlayer takedown processes. The BBC expressed regret and committed to learning from the incident to prevent future occurrences.

articles.title: Cardi B's Credit Card Fraud Case
articles.summary: Rapper Cardi B has reported being a victim of credit card fraud, with thieves allegedly spending $60,000 on her American Express card. The incident occurred while she was on her Little Miss Drama tour, and she disclosed it in an Instagram live video watched by over 24,000 fans. According to Cardi B, her card went missing two weeks prior, and it was used to make a $40,000 purchase at Saks Fifth Avenue for designer clothes and a $20,000 purchase at an Apple Store before the card was frozen. She explained that her phone receives notifications for every transaction, which alerted her to the suspicious activity. Since she wasn't shopping much during her tour, the large purchases raised red flags, and she obtained CCTV footage showing four men using her card. Cardi B, whose real name is Belcalis Marlenis Almánzar and net worth exceeds $88 million, emphasized her vigilance with finances, stating she doesn't tolerate any unauthorized spending. She is calling for the perpetrators to be jailed and has contacted American Express to verify the claims. This case highlights issues of financial security and celebrity vulnerability to fraud.

articles.title: Channel 4 Cancels Bake Off Episode Amid Allegations Against DJ Scott Mills
articles.summary: Channel 4 has announced that it will not broadcast a celebrity episode of The Great British Bake Off featuring DJ Scott Mills. This decision comes after Mills was dismissed by the BBC last month over a historical allegation of serious sexual offences. Although Mills cooperated with a prior police investigation that did not lead to criminal charges, Channel 4 stated it carefully considered the circumstances and deemed it inappropriate to air the episode at this time due to the seriousness of the accusations. As a result, an alternative episode of Celebrity GBBO will be shown instead. Additionally, Mills has been removed from Boyzone's upcoming concerts at Emirates Stadium, where he was scheduled as a special guest DJ, though it is unclear if this was voluntary or imposed. He has also stepped back from his role as an ambassador for the MS Society UK, a charity he supported for over a decade after his mother's diagnosis with multiple sclerosis. The charity is reviewing the situation while Mills takes a break from his duties. This series of actions highlights how allegations can impact a public figure's media appearances and charitable involvements, even without formal legal consequences.

articles.title: India's Aviation Challenge: The Widebody Aircraft Shortage
articles.summary: Willie Walsh, the incoming chief executive of IndiGo, has criticized India's aviation sector for having only 50 widebody aircraft, describing it as a 'scandal'. In an interview with the BBC, Walsh emphasized that widebody aircraft, which are larger, twin-aisle planes designed for long-haul and international routes, are crucial for India's growth. With a population of 1.4 billion and increasing demand for air travel, the current fleet is insufficient to meet connectivity needs. Walsh, who will join IndiGo in August after serving as director general of the International Air Transport Association, avoided commenting on IndiGo's recent crisis, where the airline canceled about 4,500 flights due to new pilot duty regulations. Instead, he focused on the broader issue of India's aviation landscape, noting its strong growth but highlighting the gap in long-haul capacity. He argued that India should have a much larger widebody fleet to capitalize on its demographic and geographic advantages, pointing out that the limited number of these aircraft hinders non-stop international travel and overall market expansion.

articles.title: US Refineries Process Venezuelan Oil After Sanctions Ease
articles.summary: The Minerva Gloria, a large ship, is delivering Venezuelan crude oil to the United States, marking a significant shift after US sanctions were lifted. Venezuela possesses the world's largest oil reserves, but exports had declined due to underinvestment and sanctions under former President Nicolás Maduro. Following Maduro's capture and policy changes, Venezuela's oil exports have rebounded, exceeding one million barrels per day recently. Chevron, a major US oil company, is now importing substantial amounts of Venezuelan oil, averaging 250,000 barrels daily, with plans to increase this. This oil is processed at Chevron's refinery in Pascagoula, Mississippi, which is designed for heavy crude like Venezuela's. Venezuelan oil is heavy, high in sulfur, and cheaper, making it suitable for producing diesel, gasoline, and jet fuel. The increased imports could boost US oil supply, potentially lowering gasoline prices. While Chevron is the only US company extracting oil in Venezuela, others are purchasing it from local producers. The US has many refineries that handle heavy crude efficiently, reducing reliance on Middle Eastern oil.

articles.title: Wireless Festival Cancelled Due to UK Government Ban on Kanye West Over Antisemitic Remarks
articles.summary: The Wireless Festival, scheduled for this summer in London, has been cancelled after headliner Kanye West, now known as Ye, was denied entry to the UK by the government. The Home Office refused his Electronic Travel Authorisation application, stating his presence would not be conducive to the public good, due to his history of antisemitic, racist, and pro-Nazi comments. In recent years, West has made inflammatory statements, such as threatening Jewish people and praising Hitler, and released offensive content like songs and merchandise. The festival organizers announced the cancellation, offering refunds to all ticket holders, and acknowledged the impact of antisemitism. They noted that multiple stakeholders were consulted during the booking process without initial concerns. West has apologized and expressed a desire to engage with the UK's Jewish community. Despite the controversy, presale tickets sold out rapidly, highlighting the event's popularity. This incident reflects ongoing debates about free speech, public safety, and accountability in the entertainment industry.
